coredata.dc:description: Disease-related proteins are biomarkers for disease diagnosis and treatment, e.g. amyloidogenic proteins are highly related to neurodegenerative diseases. Detection and monitoring of disease-related proteins is significant for the biomedical research and early disease diagnosis. Thus, the rapid, sensitive and low-cost approach is highly desired. For this purpose, herein, a novel dual-element sensor array was constructed based on two carbon nanodots (CDs). The CDs were prepared using citric acid and Congo red as carbon source by deliberately doping with two different oxidants, i.e. ammonium persulfate and hydrogen peroxide. The fluorescence of CDs could be efficiently and selectively quenched by proteins to different degrees, in which electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions might co-contribute to the sensing process. In the differentiability test, the established sensor array could accurately discriminate four amyloidogenic proteins and two serum proteins with a classification accuracy of 100%. The applicability could be extended to proteins quantification and mixture proteins discrimination. Typically, fibrillation stages of amyloidogenic protein (α-synuclein as example) in artificial cerebrospinal fluid were differentiated by the array, which was meaningful to illustrate the process of neurodegenerative diseases. Moreover, the sensor array successfully distinguished cancer patients (liver and breast cancers) and healthy people based on μL-level of serum sample, demonstrating its potential for fast screen of cancer at large scale. The constructed sensor array based on the oxidants doped CDs was promising for disease-related proteins discrimination, quantification and progressive fibrillation monitoring, and afforded a powerful discriminative and adaptive tool for biomedical research and disease diagnosis.

head.ce:title: Array-based sensing of amyloidogenic proteins and discrimination of cancer by using different oxidants doped carbon nanodots as fluorescent probes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Disease-related proteins are biomarkers for disease diagnosis and treatment, e.g. amyloidogenic proteins are highly related to neurodegenerative diseases. Detection and monitoring of disease-related proteins is significant for the biomedical research and early disease diagnosis. Thus, the rapid, sensitive and low-cost approach is highly desired. For this purpose, herein, a novel dual-element sensor array was constructed based on two carbon nanodots (CDs). The CDs were prepared using citric acid and Congo red as carbon source by deliberately doping with two different oxidants, i.e. ammonium persulfate and hydrogen peroxide. The fluorescence of CDs could be efficiently and selectively quenched by proteins to different degrees, in which electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions might co-contribute to the sensing process. In the differentiability test, the established sensor array could accurately discriminate four amyloidogenic proteins and two serum proteins with a classification accuracy of 100%. The applicability could be extended to proteins quantification and mixture proteins discrimination. Typically, fibrillation stages of amyloidogenic protein (α-synuclein as example) in artificial cerebrospinal fluid were differentiated by the array, which was meaningful to illustrate the process of neurodegenerative diseases. Moreover, the sensor array successfully distinguished cancer patients (liver and breast cancers) and healthy people based on μL-level of serum sample, demonstrating its potential for fast screen of cancer at large scale. The constructed sensor array based on the oxidants doped CDs was promising for disease-related proteins discrimination, quantification and progressive fibrillation monitoring, and afforded a powerful discriminative and adaptive tool for biomedical research and disease diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Neurodegenerative diseases, like Alzheimer's disease (AD), Parkinson's disease (PD) and Huntington disease etc., are afflicting more than 50 million people worldwide nowadays [1]. According to the World Health Organization report, neurodegenerative diseases will overtake cancer to be the second cause of human death in the next 20 years [2]. Thus, it is of significance to develop reliable method for biomedical research and early diagnosis of these diseases. Amyloidogenic proteins, typically including amyloid-β 1–42 (Aβ42) and α-synuclein (α-syn) which aggregate into highly ordered amyloid fibril and finally present as amyloid deposits in brain, are reckoned as the hallmark of AD and PD, respectively [1,3]. Besides, islet amyloid polypeptide (IAPP) and insulin (Ins) are also claimed to form amyloid fibril associated with type 2 diabetes [4]. Hence, to identify and monitor these proteins would afford abundant information for biomedical research and early diagnosis. Up to now, the extensively used detection methods for disease-related proteins included enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) based on specific antibody recognition (“lock-key”) [5], gel electrophoresis [6] as well as mass spectrometry [7]. However, high threshold of these approaches, such as high production cost and instability of specific antibody, time-consuming tedious operation, expensive instrument and demanding expertise [8,9], encouraged researchers to explore more simple and low-cost methods while assuring the sensitivity and selectivity. Nevertheless, the availability of such approaches was still limited, especially for amyloidogenic proteins analysis [10].

The chemical nose/tongue-mimic sensor array could be an efficient strategy to reach the goal of simultaneous analysis of multiple proteins [11]. In the approach, different interactions of proteins with a receptor array would generate a unique signal output pattern (“fingerprint”) for protein identification by means of statistical method (e.g. linear discriminant analysis (LDA) and principal component analysis) [9]. To date, various sensors including fluorescence responsive organic molecules (e.g. aggregation-induced emission molecules, conjugated polymers and organic salts) [12–15], gold nanoclusters [16–18] and quantum dots [19] were developed to construct the protein-responsive sensor array. Besides, to improve the discrimination ability, different combined systems taking advantage of metal ions, metal nanoparticles, protein-binding dyes (e.g. bromophenol blue) or aptamers with fluorescence responsive or absorbance signal output probes were smartly constructed [8,20,21]. Although nonspecific sensing elements were allowed to construct the sensor array, to ensure the efficiency of the sensor array, the sensing elements for protein sensing with maximal discrimination capability and high sensitivity, as well as minimal numbers of elements and rapid responsive rate were still highly desired, especially for diagnostic applications.

Fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs) with multiple superior optical properties, such as excellent light stability, high quantum yield (QY), as well as low toxicity, good biocompatibility, facile and low-cost preparation and environment-friendliness, were broadly studied in sensing field [22,23]. However, it is still a challenge to design specific CDs for biomacromolecules recognition in spite of their tunable optical properties and selectivities. The limited reports about sensing proteins by CDs were unexceptionally in light of antibody or aptamer of high cost and uncertain modification efficiency [24–26]. To avoid these tedious modifications and reduce high cost, the sensor array based on CDs as the sensor elements could be an alternative choice, as it only requires discrimination ability instead of specific selectivity. The dilemma when constructing the sensor array using CDs was that, for examples, the unsatisfactory sensitivity, tedious construction elements (e.g. employing multiple CDs as the sensing elements or utilizing extra multiple metal ions to increase interaction sites) and unmentioned selectivity for potential interferes besides proteins [27–29]. Therefore, it is of great value to construct the chemical nose/tongue-mimic sensor array by highly sensitive and selective CDs with low-cost and simple preparation for proteins recognition, especially for the purpose of early disease diagnosis.

In this study, for the recognition of disease-related proteins, a dual-elements CDs-based sensor array for proteins pattern recognition was established. The protein sensing CDs were prepared via a simple one-pot method using cheap chemicals (citric acid, Congo red and specific oxidant). The oxidant was crucial for both the fluorescence properties (especially quantum yield) of obtained CDs and the response performance for proteins. Based on the proposed two different oxidants doped CDs, the sensor array was constructed to identify and discriminate six proteins (containing four amyloidogenic proteins and two serum proteins) based on the fluorescence quenching effect. Furthermore, the sensor array was attempted to discriminate different fibrillation stages of amyloidogenic protein (α-syn as example) in artificial cerebrospinal fluid (aCSF), and cancer patients (liver cancer and breast cancer) from healthy people. The study suggested the sensor array of promising discrimination of amyloidogenic proteins and fibrillation stages, preliminary fast and large-scale screen of liver and breast cancers, guiding further related clinical examination.

Ins (from bovine) was obtained from the Cool Chemistry Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was purchased from the Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Recombinant human α-syn was expressed in Escherichia coli [30]. Aβ42 and IAPP were purchased from the Lifetein Biotechnology (Beijing, China). Immunoglobulin G (IgG) was obtained from the Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid, Congo red, ammonium persulfate (APS), H2O2 (30% aqueous solution), thioflavin T (ThT), hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), tetramethylammonium bromide (TMAB), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), polyethyleneimine (70 kDa), polyethylene polyamine, tetraethylenepentamine, ethylenediamine, oleylamine and oleic acid were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffer solution (PB) was prepared by NaH2PO4·2H2O and Na2HPO4·12H2O to required concentration and pH. 1 × PBS (phosphate buffered saline) was prepared by dissolving 0.8 g NaCl (137 mM), 0.02 g KH2PO4 (1.5 mM), 0.351 g Na2HPO4·12H2O (9.8 mM) and 0.02 g KCl (2.7 mM) in 100 mL ultrapure water. aCSF was prepared referring to previous work [31], which contained NaCl (124 mM), KCl (4 mM), CaCl2 (1 mM), NaH2PO4·2H2O (1.24 mM), MgSO4 (1.3 mM), NaHCO3 (26 mM) and D-glucose·H2O (10 mM). Ultrapure water was produced by a Heal Fore NW system (Shanghai, China).

The CDs were prepared by a heating reflux method. Typically, 1.5 g citric acid, 5 mg Congo red and specific oxidant, i.e. 50 mg APS or 22 μL H2O2, were dissolved in 5 mL water in a 25 mL flask. The reaction was heated from room temperature to 180 °C in oil bath with magnetic stirring, and held at 180 °C for 4 h under refluxing. Afterwards, the product was cooled down naturally and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 8 min, and the supernatant was collected and kept in the dark for further use. To investigate the influence of the oxidant on the fluorescence properties of CDs and the response performance for proteins, different types and concentrations of oxidants were studied to generate the CDs, as listed in Table 1 .

The transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images of CDs were captured by a Talos F200X electron microscope (FEI, Netherlands). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic (FT-IR) analysis was conducted by an AVATAR 360 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurements were performed with an ESCALAB Xi+ (Thermo Fischer, USA) using Al Kα radiation (hv = 1486.6 eV) as X-ray source with a base pressure of 8 × 10−10 Pa. The UV absorption and fluorescence spectra were acquired by a Lambda 365 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) and an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), respectively. The fluorescence lifetime decay curves were recorded by a Fluorescence Lifetime Spectrometer with Felix 32 system (Photon Technology International, Canada).

The fluorescence QY of CDs were measured referring to previous work by using quinine sulfate as the reference, of which the QYr is 54% at 360 nm in 0.1 M H2SO4 [32]. Typically, specific CDs and quinine sulfate were diluted to six different concentrations with ultrapure water and 0.1 M H2SO4 solution, respectively. The absorbance value of each solution at 360 nm should be below 0.1 to minimize the self-absorption effect. Then the solutions were subjected to fluorescence measurement at excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The QYCDs was calculated according to Eq. (1),

QYCDs= (QY r k c ƞ c
2)/(k r ƞ r
2) (1)

where the subscript c and r represent CDs and quinine sulfate, respectively; k denotes the slope of fitted linear curve of integrated fluorescence area versus absorbance value; ƞ is the refractive index of solvent (ƞ of ultrapure water and 0.1 M H2SO4 solution are both 1.33) [32].

A series of target proteins stock aqueous solution (1 mg/mL) were first prepared and stored at 4 °C. Two sensing elements (APS-2-CDs and H2O2-2-CDs as listed in Table 1) were optimized to construct the sensor array. Typically, APS-2-CDs (30 μL) or H2O2-2-CDs (20 μL) solution was mixed with 1 mL of 10 μg/mL target proteins in 10 mM PB (pH 7.4). The solution was then subjected to the fluorescence measurement at the maximum excitation wavelength (
λ ex
max
) and emission wavelength (
λ em
max
) of APS-2-CDs (
λ ex
max
/
λ em
max
355/430 nm) and H2O2-2-CDs (
λ ex
max
/
λ em
max
365/436 nm). Six replicate tests were performed for each protein sample. The value (F0/F) was then calculated to obtain the fluorescence response, where F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensity of the specific CDs in the absence and presence of target protein, respectively. At last, data were processed using LDA in IBM SPSS Statistics 25 (International Business Machines Corporation, USA). The canonical score plot was obtained by using the calculated factor (1) and factor (2) as x and y axis, respectively.

To estimate the applicability of the constructed sensor array in actual matrix, urine from healthy volunteer was collected and centrifuged at 4,500 rpm for 10 min to remove the insoluble matrix. Six proteins (Ins, BSA, α-syn, Aβ42, IAPP and IgG) were individually spiked into the urine sample (diluted with 50% PB) to ultimate concentration of 5 μg/mL, which were used for proteins discrimination with the same detection condition and data processing as above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of α-syn at different fibrillation stages in aCSF
ce:section.ce:para: To induce the fibrillation of α-syn in vitro, 600 μL of 1 mg/mL α-syn (in 1 × PBS, pH 7.4) was incubated at 37 °C for 120 h on a rotary shaker. During the incubation, 50 μL of the incubation sample was collected at the predetermined time interval and immediately stored at −20 °C. To monitor the fibrillation process of α-syn, the standard ThT measurements were conducted to the collected sample. Specifically, ThT (10 μM) was mixed with the collected protein sample diluted 200 times in 10 mM PB, and the fluorescence intensity was then recorded at λex/λem of 430/490 nm, the result of which was used as a reference for the sensor array study. Typically, to discriminate the different fibrillation stages of α-syn protein in aCSF by the constructed sensor array, APS-2-CDs (30 μL) or H2O2-2-CDs (20 μL) was mixed with 1 mL of protein sample diluted 1,000 times in aCSF, which was collected at the specific time (0, 24, 48, 84 and 108 h). The fluorescence measurement and data processing were conducted as Section 2.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of cancer patients and healthy people by CDs array-based sensing system
ce:section.ce:para:
Serum samples of liver cancer patients (5 samples), breast cancer patients (5 samples) and healthy people (10 samples) were obtained from the Union Hospital of Huazhong University of Science and Technology (Wuhan, China), in which the cancer patients were diagnosed by the related clinical criteria. The basic inclusion criteria for both cancer patients and healthy people were: all genders, aged 45–75 years. The specific inclusion criteria for cancer patients, typically, were (1) for the patients who were primarily diagnosed as liver cancer but without surgical resection: liver space-occupying lesions diagnosed by computed tomography or magnetic resonance imaging or ultrasonography; (2) for the patients who were diagnosed as breast cancer: invasive breast carcinoma diagnosed according to the result of needle biopsy, no surgical resection or after surgical resection but breast cancer metastasis. All the tests were approved by the ethics committee of Huazhong University of Science and Technology and performed in compliance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines. For a typical detection process, 1 μL specific serum sample was added into 1 mL array CDs solution (30 μL/mL of APS-2-CDs or 20 μL/mL of H2O2-2-CDs in 10 mM PB, pH 7.4). Then the fluorescence responses (F0/F) were recorded at
λ ex
max
/
λ em
max
355/430 nm for APS-2-CDs and
λ ex
max
/
λ em
max
365/436 nm for H2O2-2-CDs, where F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensity without and with the addition of serum sample, respectively. Each serum sample was tested in six replicates and the raw data were finally processed using LDA in IBM SPSS Statistics 25 as Section 2.4.

The protein-sensing CDs were fabricated by using citric acid as the main carbon source, and deliberately introducing Congo red as another carbon source with heteroatom doping (N and S), which was specific for protein secondary structures [33]. Particularly, in our preliminary experiment, oxidant was found to be crucial for the fluorescence property of the prepared CDs, as well as their response for proteins. Hence, two common oxidants (APS and H2O2) were representatively employed to study the influence of doping amounts on the properties of the obtained CDs. As revealed in Table 1, compared with the QY of c-CDs (i.e. 1.01%) without oxidant doping, the QY of the other CDs with addition of small amount of APS or H2O2 was significantly enhanced. Specifically, with the increasing concentration of APS, QYAPS-n-CDs gradually increased and reached the maximum of 12.60% with the addition of 10 mg/mL APS (APS-2-CDs), while an overdose of APS (20–100 mg/mL) markedly decreased the QY. As for the case of H2O2-n-CDs, the QY reached 34.41% in the presence of 4.4 μL/mL H2O2 (H2O2-2-CDs), and further increased to 43.63% when 200 μL/mL H2O2 was added. The results disclosed that the appropriate oxidant could favor the enhancement of the QY, while the concentration of oxidant could be determinant. To further investigate the influence of oxidants on the obtained CDs, three typical CDs, i.e. APS-2-CDs, H2O2-2-CDs and c-CDs, were characterized and compared.

All the three CDs, i.e. APS-2-CDs, H2O2-2-CDs and c-CDs, exhibited well-dispersed quasi spheres, as shown in Fig. 1 A. According to the statistical results of 85 particles of individual CDs, the sizes of APS-2-CDs, H2O2-2-CDs and c-CDs were 2.0 ± 0.4 nm, 2.1 ± 0.5 nm and 2.5 ± 0.4 nm, respectively. Besides, the high-resolution TEM images in the insets of Fig. 1A revealed the typical crystalline structure of graphitic carbon in all the three CDs, with lattice spacing of 0.23 nm, 0.21 nm and 0.20 nm corresponding to APS-2-CDs, H2O2-2-CDs and c-CDs, respectively [23,34,35]. As can be seen, no significant morphological and crystalline differences of the obtained CDs with the specific doped oxidant were observed.

Furthermore, the chemical functional groups and surface chemical composition of the three CDs were further investigated. In Fig. 1B, FT-IR analysis indicated that APS-2-CDs, H2O2-2-CDs and c-CDs shared almost the same absorption peaks, which were identified as νO-H/N-H 3440 cm−1; νC-H 2926 cm−1; νC=O 1724 cm−1; νC=C 1626 cm−1; νC-N 1400 cm−1; νC-O 1226 cm−1; νS=O 1122 cm−1 and 1050 cm−1; γ=C-H 890 cm−1 and νC-S 602 cm−1 [36–38]. It could be speculated that the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in citric acid as well as the amino, sulfonic groups and aromatic ring in Congo red may remain in the obtained three CDs. Furthermore, the full XPS scan spectra (Fig. 1C-I) disclosed the existence of C1s (299 eV), O1s (546 eV), N1s (411 eV) and S2p (176 eV) in the three CDs. Specifically, the high-resolution C1s spectra of APS-2-CDs and H2O2-2-CDs both possessed four peaks at 284.7 eV, 285.8–286.3 eV, 287.1 eV and 288.8 eV, corresponding to C–C/C=C, C-O/C-S, C=O and O-C=O [38,39], respectively (Fig. 1C-II); whereas, C=O peak which might be resulted from the oxidation by oxidants disappeared in c-CDs. In addition, the high-resolution O1s spectra of three CDs all had two signals at 531.7 eV and 532.7 eV which ascribed to C=O and C-O [32], respectively (Fig. 1C-III), where the C=O signal in c-CDs could be from the carboxyl corresponding to O-C=O signal in C1s spectrum. Both the high-resolution C1s and O1s spectra revealed the higher oxidation state on the surface of the two oxidants doped CDs, i.e. APS-2-CDs and H2O2-2-CDs, as C=O signal in these two CDs was obviously higher than c-CDs, which coincided well with the previous report about H2O2-treated CDs [35]. Besides, the high-resolution N1s spectra indicated that the three CDs all possessed pyridinic N (C-N-C) at 399.5 eV and N–H at 401.5 eV [40], where APS-2-CDs and H2O2-2-CDs had larger relative amount of pyridinic N than c-CDs (Fig. 1C-IV). Furthermore, the high-resolution S2p spectra disclosed the existence of the phenyl-SO3 (at 168.1–168.4 eV) in all the three CDs, while the sulfide (C-S-C, at 163.5–163.8 eV) signal emerged in APS-2-CDs and H2O2-2-CDs (Fig. 1C-V) [38,41]. In sum, XPS result disclosed the higher content of C=O, C-N-C and C-S-C groups in oxidants doped CDs than those of c-CDs.

As reported, the photoluminescence mechanisms of CDs mainly included defect state emission (e.g. the surface energy traps) and intrinsic state emission (e.g. electron-hole recombination, quantum size confinement effect) [42]. It was speculated that the increased amount of C=O groups in APS-2-CDs and H2O2-2-CDs may affect the surface defect states [35], and the enhanced amount of C-N-C and C-S-C groups which benefited to form conjugated π electron system in carbon backbone may affect the electron-hole recombination [42]. Under the dual influences, the oxidants doped CDs were expected to exhibit superior fluorescence performance than c-CDs in terms of high QY. More importantly, the increased C-N-C and C-S-C groups in APS-2-CDs and H2O2-2-CDs could endow the CDs with more hydrophobic interaction sites in contrast to c-CDs, which would be a favor to sense proteins in light of the hydrophobic interaction between ligand and protein as discussed below.

The optical properties including absorption and fluorescence spectra of the three CDs were explored in Fig. S1. Under the natural light, APS-2-CDs, H2O2-2-CDs and c-CDs appeared distinctively different colors, i.e. yellowish-brown, light purple and rose red, respectively. Under the same concentration, APS-2-CDs and H2O2-2-CDs exhibited greatly strengthened absorbance at 290 nm and 365 nm compared to c-CDs, which were assigned to the π-π* transition in the carbon cores and the n-π* transition of C=O, respectively (Fig. S1A-C) [43,35]. The result indicated that the transition probability of chromophore (e.g. C=O) in oxidant-doped CDs might be enhanced [44], as characterized by XPS. In contrast, the peak at 520 nm of c-CDs ascribed to the long conjugate structure of Congo red, almost disappeared in APS-2-CDs and H2O2-2-CDs. In addition, APS-2-CDs and H2O2-2-CDs both emitted bright blue fluorescence under λex of 365 nm, while the fluorescence of c-CDs was feeble for its low QY. A slight excitation dependent photoluminescence with redshift was observed for APS-2-CDs and H2O2-2-CDs, while a slight blueshift occurred in c-CDs (Fig. S1D-F). This phenomenon implied that different fluorescence centers may be present which resulted in different energy level transition in oxidants doped CDs comparing to c-CDs [22].

In summary, the incorporation of oxidants (APS and H2O2) could markedly enhance the QY of the obtained CDs, which depended largely on the amount of oxidant. The increased C=O, C-N-C and C-S-C groups on the surface of APS-2-CDs and H2O2-2-CDs could be responsible for the improvement of QY and sensing performances in contrast to c-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protein sensing by APS-n-CDs and H2O2-n-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In our preliminary experiment, both APS-n-CDs and H2O2-n-CDs had obvious response for proteins compared to the none-oxidant doping one (i.e. c-CDs), and the doping amounts of APS and H2O2 were found to largely effect their sensing performance. In order to systematically optimize the amount of doped oxidants for the obtained CDs in respect of proteins sensing performance, the fluorescence responses (F0/F) of APS-n-CDs and H2O2-n-CDs (as listed in Table 1) for six model proteins with relatively low concentration (i.e. 1 μg/mL) were individually investigated, including four typical amyloidogenic proteins (α-syn, Aβ42, Ins and IAPP) and other two common serum proteins (BSA and IgG) as listed in Table S1. As indicated in Fig. 2 A, the six model proteins quenched the fluorescence of APS-/H2O2-n-CDs to different degrees. In detail, for both APS-n-CDs and H2O2-n-CDs, the overall F0/F values for proteins sensing increased and then decreased corresponding to the enhancing amount of oxidant, where the overall sensitive response for each protein occurred in the case of APS-2-CDs and H2O2-2-CDs. Specifically, the fluorescence spectra and photos of APS-2-CDs and H2O2-2-CDs for the six tested proteins (1 μg/mL) clearly reflected the efficient fluorescence quenching of CDs by the tested proteins (Fig. 2B and C).

Additionally, Fig. 2D demonstrated that the fluorescence responsive rates of APS-2-CDs and H2O2-2-CDs towards proteins were rapid (within 1 min), taking α-syn and Aβ42 (both of 10 μg/mL) as examples. Therefore, the incubation time needed no deliberate control. Furthermore, the selectivity test demonstrated that APS-2-CDs and H2O2-2-CDs had high specificity for protein sensing in contrast to various possible potential interferes (e.g. amino acids and metal cations) in biological sample (Fig. 2E). It was worthy to mention that though L-lysine (50 μg/mL) exhibited relatively obvious quenching towards APS-2-CDs and H2O2-2-CDs, the response became negligible when the concentration of L-lysine lowered down to 10 μg/mL or lower, which was still ten times higher concentration of the tested proteins (1 μg/mL) compared with Fig. 2A.

Thus, in light of the phenomenon that the fluorescence of APS-2-CDs and H2O2-2-CDs could be efficiently quenched to different degrees by model proteins with rapid responsive rate and high selectivity, by means of constructing the sensor array with the two sensing elements, an efficient antibody-free method can be developed to selectively and rapidly recognize and discriminate proteins.

To further explore the possible sensing mechanism of the two CDs for proteins, fluorescence lifetime of APS-2-CDs, H2O2-2-CDs and their incubation with proteins (using Aβ42 and α-syn as examples, representing as APS-2-CDs + Aβ42, APS-2-CDs + α-syn, H2O2-2-CDs + Aβ42, H2O2-2-CDs + α-syn) were firstly measured in Fig. S2A and B. The fluorescence decay curves of only APS-2-CDs and H2O2-2-CDs both fitted well with the single exponential function, obtaining the lifetimes of 2.60 ns and 2.33 ns, respectively. However, the lifetimes of APS-2-CDs + Aβ42 and APS-2-CDs + α-syn changed a little, calculated as 2.89 ns and 3.09 ns, respectively. Similarly, there was almost no change of decay curves for H2O2-2-CDs after incubation with the tested proteins, where the lifetimes of H2O2-2-CDs + Aβ42 and H2O2-2-CDs + α-syn were calculated as 2.47 ns and 2.58 ns, respectively. These results implied that the tested proteins did not change the lifetime of excited state of CDs, and may interact with ground state of CDs [45]. Furthermore, the absorption spectra of APS-2-CDs + Aβ42/α-syn and H2O2-2-CDs + Aβ42/α-syn both changed in contrast to the respective CDs, with the strengthened absorbance or redshift of absorption peaks (Fig. S2C and D), which further demonstrated the interaction between proteins and ground state of CDs rather than its excited state [46], suggesting static quenching for the quenching process.

Therefore, to investigate the interaction forces between the two CDs with proteins in detail, series of control CDs were prepared by subtraction of one raw material (citric acid or Congo red), and their sensing performance towards proteins (using Ins and BSA as examples) were tested. The comparison results in Table S2 manifested that, in addition to oxidants, the main carbon sources, i.e. citric acid and Congo red, were both responsible for the detectability of CDs towards proteins. Besides, another attempt to replace citric acid by ethylenediamine in the preparation of APS-2-CDs or H2O2-2-CDs were performed; however, silent fluorescence responses were acquired (data not shown). Above trials inspired us to speculate that the carboxyl from citric acid in CDs responsible for the electrostatic interaction with proteins may be significant for sensing. In addition, the aromatic ring originating from Congo red in CDs suggested that the hydrophobic interaction may be another important interaction. Besides, doped oxidants not only enhanced the QY of CDs (Table 1), but also enriched the hydrophobic sites for enhanced C-N-C and C-S-C groups on the surface of CDs as characterized by XPS, which could be equally indispensable.

To further support our speculation about the two vital interactions, i.e. electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions, cationic surfactant and alkyl amines compounds simultaneously possessing positively charged groups (quaternary ammonium or amino groups) and hydrophobic groups (alkyl groups), as well as their corresponding control compounds without positively charged groups or alkyl groups, were chosen to test the detectability of APS-2-CDs and H2O2-2-CDs. As revealed in Fig. S2E and F, the cationic surfactant (CTAB, 1 μg/mL) could effectively quench the fluorescence of CDs, while the anionic surfactant (SDS, 50 μg/mL) did not exert any quenching ability. Besides, contrast to CTAB, TMAB (50 μg/mL) without the long alkyl chain also had no quenching effect for CDs. As a further demonstration, alkyl amines compounds (1 μg/mL), i.e. polyethyleneimine, polyethylene polyamine, tetraethylenepentamine and ethylenediamine with shortened alkyl chain length in the order, gave the lessened F0/F of CDs. Besides, oleylamine and oleic acid, with the same alkyl chain while oppositely charged terminal groups (amino and carboxyl, respectively), exhibited distinctively different quenching effects for CDs. Specifically, oleylamine (1 μg/mL) could effectively quench the fluorescence of CDs while oleic acid (50 μg/mL) could barely. These comparison tests indicated that the positively charged and hydrophobic groups in analytes were indispensable to obtain the effective quenching response of CDs. It should be noted that the effective sensing of proteins by the two CDs occurred at acidic condition, resulting from the excess citric acid in the obtained CDs solution. The low pH condition (i.e. pH of 2.5–2.7 for APS-2-CDs (∼30 μL/mL) and H2O2-2-CDs (∼20 μL/mL) in PB) could lead to the protonation of positively charged residues in protein, which guaranteed the electrostatic interaction between CDs and protein. Overall, as the tested surfactants and alkyl amines were usually not present in the biological system, specific sensing and identification of proteins by our proposed CDs-based sensor array could be still guaranteed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protein discrimination by the sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: Four amyloidogenic proteins (α-syn, Aβ42, Ins and IAPP) and two common serum proteins (BSA and IgG) as listed in Table S1 with different sizes (MW of 4.51–151.33 kDa), charges (pI of 4.67–9.83), percentages of positively charged residues (arginine and lysine) and hydrophobicity (GRAVY from −0.429 to 0.205) were used as model proteins to investigate the discrimination ability of the constructed sensor array. The dual-channel pattern response for the six tested proteins (10 μg/mL) based on APS-2-CDs and H2O2-2-CDs was illustrated as a “fingerprint” map (Fig. 3 A and B), in which the signal readout of each protein was unique. A sensor array with 2 elements × 6 proteins × 6 replicates was herein established. To further statistically investigate the fluorescence response pattern of CDs-based array against different target proteins, classical LDA was performed to transform the raw data of matrix into canonical factors in the two-dimensional plot (Fig. 3C). It can be seen that the six different proteins were well clustered in individual groups with a classification accuracy of 100%. This result demonstrated that the constructed dual-elements array possessed excellent discrimination capability for the six target proteins.

Furthermore, for the fluorescence response of the CDs for 10 μg/mL proteins were remarkable, the sensor array was further investigated to evaluate its ability for low-concentration proteins discrimination. As presented in Fig. 3D and E, the sensor array exhibited good discrimination performance for 1 μg/mL even 0.2 μg/mL (1.32–44.31 nM) tested proteins with 100% identification accuracy, demonstrating the high sensitivity of the sensor array for detection and differentiation of the studied proteins.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protein quantification and mixture protein discrimination
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the quantification ability of the sensor array for proteins, the identification and discrimination of Aβ42 with different concentrations was investigated, which is one of the significant biomarkers for AD [10,47]. In Fig. 4 A, the LDA plot for Aβ42 with various concentrations (0.2–10 μg/mL) showed well-clustered distinct groups without overlap, indicating good discrimination ability of the sensor array for Aβ42 of different concentrations. Moreover, because the first discriminant factor (factor 1) accounted for 99.5% of the total variability in the data, a linearity was obtained simplistically by using factor 1 to quantify Aβ42 with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9855, as depicted in Fig. 4B.

Additionally, it was also a challenge to distinguish complex protein mixture by sensor array [18]. Herein, series of binary protein mixture with different mass ratios of Aβ42 associated with AD and α-syn associated with PD were discriminated by the developed sensor array, where the total protein concentration remained as low as 1 μg/mL. As disclosed in Fig. 4C, a good discrimination performance and a certain recognition pattern for the five different ratios of binary protein mixture were obtained. Overall, it can be prospected that the designed sensor array was capable of quantifying proteins as well as distinguishing the mixture of proteins.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of α-syn protein at different fibrillation stages in aCSF
ce:section.ce:para: As reported, neurodegenerative diseases such as AD and PD are commonly characterized of pathological amyloid deposits in brain [3]. The misfolding and aggregation of the intrinsically amyloidogenic proteins into highly ordered amyloid fibrils is greatly related to neurodegenerative diseases [1]. The aggregation process of amyloidogenic proteins was usually reckoned as the morphology conversion from soluble monomers to oligomers and then to insoluble pre-fibrils finally as amyloid fibrils, along with the increasing β-sheet structures corresponding to the enhanced hydrophobicity [33]. As discussed above, the hydrophobic interaction was involved for our developed array sensor to discriminate proteins, thus the different fibrillation stages of amyloidogenic proteins based on the different hydrophobic interactions would be expected to be detected and monitored. Herein, taking α-syn as an example in Fig. 4D, the kinetic aggregation process of α-syn in vitro was first monitored by the recognized ThT method. It can be seen that α-syn begun to aggregate after 12 h, and reached the platform after 96 h. To investigate the identification ability of the sensor array for different fibrillation stages of α-syn, samples collected at five different time points (0, 24, 48, 84 and 108 h) in the aggregation process were tested. To mimic the physiological condition, the detection process of aggregated α-syn by sensor array was carried on in aCSF, which contained various inorganic salts and glucose. As revealed in Fig. 4E, the five groups corresponding to α-syn of different aggregated times were well clustered without any overlap, indicating that the sensor array could identify different fibrillation stages of α-syn (e.g. monomer, oligomer and fibril). More importantly, the canonical factor 1 (98.1%) showed clear correlation with the aggregation time of α-syn, endowing the sensor array with the ability to monitor the fibrillation process especially at the early stage, which would be appreciated in the early intervention of disease.

Furthermore, the applicability of the developed sensor platform in real complex matrix was evaluated by a trail of discrimination of the six model proteins (5 μg/mL) in urine (50% in PB, v/v). As revealed in Fig. 4F, the canonical score plot indicated that the sensor array showed favorable recognition and discrimination ability for the six tested proteins in urine samples with an accuracy of 97.6%, where Ins and α-syn had a little overlap resulting from the small difference of fluorescence response of the two sensing elements for them in urine matrix. In addition, 36 blind protein samples in urine matrix were identified with an identification accuracy of 88.9% (See Table S3), suggesting the practicability of our developed sensor array to discriminate proteins in real samples at low concentration.

The comparison of the protein discrimination performance between our developed sensor array with other reported fluorescence sensor arrays was listed in Table S4. Specifically, for the fluorescent organic molecules-based sensor array [12–15], it could be suffered from the relatively tedious synthesis process of the fluoroprobe, which might be an obstacle in terms of low cost and easy-availability of the probes. Besides, the selectivity of these fluoroprobes based on organic molecules towards proteins among the potential interferes was scarcely considered. In addition, various fluorescent nanodots, such as CDs, gold nanoclusters and quantum dots were also adopted to construct the proteins-responsive sensor array. However, most of them used extra metal ions, small molecules or nanoparticles, etc. to realize the proteins response by means of fluorescence turn off–on and/or turn off-off [8,9,19,21,27,28]. Despite that the reported multiple gold nanoclusters showed fluorescence turn on or turn off towards proteins, high concentration of analytical proteins (200–400 μg/mL) and long incubation time (2–3 h) were demanded [16–18], which hindered its further application. In contrast, our developed different oxidants doped CDs realized efficient and fast (1 min) detection of proteins via fluorescence turn-off signal, instead of utilizing the competitive interaction between proteins and other extra added species as mentioned above, implying the potential facile adaptability of the developed method for more applications. Furthermore, the selectivity test ensured the proposed CDs with the excellent specificity for protein sensing among other potential interferes (amino acids and metal ions). Therefore, our developed sensor array using only two sensing elements exhibited high efficiency to discriminate six proteins at a low concentration of 0.2 μg/mL, which was lower than other reported protein-responsive sensor arrays [9,13–19,21,27–29]. More particularly, our sensor array focused on the discrimination of amyloidogenic proteins and fibrillation stages related to neurodegenerative diseases, and preliminary fast and large-scale screen of liver and breast cancers (as discussed below), which could be an effective and adaptable tool for biomedical research and disease diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of cancer patients and healthy people by the sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: For cancer is the second cause of human death nowadays (higher than neurodegeneration diseases) [2], the development of point-of-care fast screen of cancer is significant for human health. Herein, serum-based discrimination of cancer patients and healthy people by our constructed sensor array was performed. The principle of discrimination was expected to be mainly based on the difference of various proteins (both types and concentrations) in serum between cancer patients and healthy people [15]. Two high-incidence cancers (liver cancer and breast cancer) were chosen to evaluate the discrimination ability of the sensor array. As demonstrated in Fig. 5 , the serum samples from the two kinds of cancer patients (10 samples) and healthy people (10 samples) were well distinguished, indicating the sensor array could effectively discriminate cancer patients and healthy people. The response signal of the sensing elements for the serum from healthy people were stable and almost indistinctive, while the response for the serum from cancer patients showed marked difference compared to healthy people (Table S5). Hopefully, by means of the constructed sensor array, preliminary high-throughput screen of liver or breast cancer patients with the merits of fast and convenient operation based on the fingertip blood sampling (for only need of μL-level serum sample) might be realized. The preliminary screen result could offer a guide for further detailed clinical examination for cancer diagnosis.

In this study, a dual-element sensor array based on carbon nanodots (CDs) was successfully constructed to sensitively and selectively detect disease-related proteins. The CDs with specificity for sensing of proteins were for the first time prepared by using citric acid and Congo red as carbon source, doped with different oxidants (APS and H2O2). It was found that a small amount of oxidant could largely enhance the fluorescence quantum yield of the obtained CDs, and the three raw materials were demonstrated to be all necessary to efficiently sense proteins in light of electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions. The array was capable of discriminating four typical amyloidogenic proteins (α-syn, Aβ42, Ins and IAPP) and two common serum proteins (BSA and IgG) at concentrations down to 0.2 μg/mL with 100% accuracy. Besides, the sensor array showed favorable applicability to quantify protein and discriminate protein mixture. The successful differentiation of fibrillation stages of amyloidogenic proteins (α-syn as example) in aCSF by the array further indicated its potential use in diagnosis of neurodegenerative diseases. Furthermore, the sensor array could distinguish cancer patients (liver and breast cancers) and healthy people by screening serums, demonstrating its value for fast screen of liver and breast cancers. The constructed simple and low-cost sensor array could be an efficient antibody-free method to identify disease-related proteins with great potential for medical diagnosis. However, there are still challenges for the clinical applications of our developed sensor array. As for the clinical samples, the dilemma would be the complex sample matrix and the individual difference between patients. Target proteins might vary in terms of the types and/or numbers in the actual clinical samples. In addition, for the purpose of identification of above two cancers in clinic, more potential influential factors such as other accompanying chronic diseases are needed to be further systematically investigated. In sum, to apply the developed sensor array to the clinical applications, database based on the massive data statistics would be necessary to afford reliable and accurate analysis.
